project_overview.project_development_objective: To strengthen the institutional and supervisory capacity of the central bank and improve the efficiency of core financial sector infrastructure
project_overview.beneficiaries: Primary: Bank of South Sudan (BoSS) and Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU). Secondary: Financial institutions, banking sector professionals, existing users of formal financial services (5.8% of adult population with financial accounts). Direct capacity building for approximately 260 BoSS staff (48% of workforce) and 28 FIU staff (68% of workforce)

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - South Sudan is classified as a fragile and conflict-affected state. The country emerged from civil war (2013-2018), remains fragile with elevated violence in several areas, approximately 2.2 million internally displaced persons, and 2.2 million refugees in neighboring countries. Formal peace agreement (R-ARCSS) signed September 2018 but implementation remains slow with transitional period extended to February 2025

key_components.description: Development of Financial Sector Development Strategy and Roadmap; capacity building for BoSS staff on financial sector policy formulation and implementation; includes sector diagnostic studies, advisory services, monitoring framework development, stakeholder consultations, and dissemination planning

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Institutional Strengthening of the Central Bank
key_components.description: Enhancement of supervisory and regulatory oversight through risk-based supervision framework; establishment of South Sudan Institute of Banking and Finance (SSIBF) for continuous learning and professional development; human resources development assessment; gender strategy development; training center upgrades

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Supporting the Development and Modernization of Key Infrastructure for the Financial Sector
key_components.description: Upgrade and customization of Core Banking System (CBS) from version 8.3 to version 9; enhancement of Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) capacity through software solutions for suspicious transaction reporting and analysis; training on AML/CFT issues

Project implementation support including hiring of project coordinator, procurement specialist, financial management specialist, environmental and social specialist; operational costs of Project Steering Committee and Technical Working Group; M&E, audits, and communications

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Bank of South Sudan (BoSS) as primary implementing agency; Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) as co-implementing entity for Component 3.2
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Two-tier governance structure: (1) Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by BoSS Governor, including MoFP, financial sector stakeholders, private sector representatives; meets quarterly to oversee implementation, provide policy guidance, ensure interagency coordination, review annual work plans. (2) Technical Working Group (TWG) chaired by First Deputy Governor of BoSS; meets quarterly for technical oversight. Project Implementation Unit (PIU) established within BoSS led by full-time project coordinator reporting to First Deputy Governor. Financial Sector Coordination Group to be established by BoSS for partner coordination
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations (November 2020); Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared; competitive international bidding for large systems investments; use of STEP tool for procurement planning and tracking; prior review of certain procurement activities regardless of value; procurement risk rated High with mitigation through PIU capacity building, training, and World Bank oversight
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: BoSS responsible for M&E using Results Framework with PDO and intermediate indicators; quarterly reporting by PIU to BoSS and World Bank; semi-annual and annual monitoring of indicators; World Bank implementation support missions at least twice yearly; periodic Implementation Status and Results Reports (ISRs); Midterm Review at approximately 30 months; Implementation Completion and Results Review at project end; beneficiary satisfaction surveys; third-party assessments for training effectiveness

identified_risks.risk_description: Highly fragile context with volatile security situation, intercommunal conflicts, and political uncertainty. Risk of frequent changes in political leadership and appointees undermining Government ownership and causing project delays. Slow implementation of R-ARCSS with transitional period extended to February 2025
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Engage with multiple levels within BoSS and key financial sector stakeholders to ensure broad buy-in and ownership; support regular coordination meetings; build flexibility into project design to allow adjustments during implementation; seek high-level political endorsement for proposed financial sector reforms

identified_risks.risk_description: High inflation risk from commodity price shocks and currency depreciation; heavy dependence on oil revenues for central government budget; high risk of debt distress (67% of GDP); weak domestic revenue mobilization; shrinking humanitarian aid budgets; vulnerability to external shocks. Real GDP contracted 2.3% in FY2021/22 and projected to stagnate at -0.4% in FY2022/23
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Continue coordination with IMF and other development partners to support Government economic management; monitor macroeconomic indicators; build flexibility into project implementation timeline

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial Sector Development Strategy does not currently exist. Risk that strategy may be knocked off course by wider political climate or special interest groups. Weak enforcement of policies and regulations by BoSS historically
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ensure project alignment with broader reforms; seek high-level political endorsement for proposed financial sector reforms; conduct wide stakeholder consultations; build consensus among key stakeholders; establish coordination mechanisms with development partners

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: First World Bank-financed project for BoSS (though implementing AfDB project); limited institutional capacity; risk of staff turnover following training investments; trained staff may be courted away by other opportunities; weak HR policies on recruitment, retention, and rotation; limited supervisory capacity (only 4-5 bank examinations per year possible with current staffing)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establish PIU with dedicated implementation capacity; significant resources dedicated to institutional strengthening; implement train-the-trainer approach to ensure broader staff benefit; address HR policies around recruitment, retention, and rotation; provide on-the-job training; leverage experience from AfDB project implementation; periodic World Bank reviews and implementation support

identified_risks.risk_description: High fiduciary risk due to BoSS having no prior World Bank project experience; overall institutional weakness across government; significant FM capacity gaps including lack of adequate budget monitoring system, weak accounting capacity, weak internal control arrangements, delays in external audit by Auditor General; high procurement risk due to absence of staff with World Bank procurement experience and inadequate procurement filing systems
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implement project through dedicated PIU with FM specialist (consultant) and project accountant; establish segregated US dollar Designated Account; prepare quarterly unaudited interim financial reports; maintain Special Account for local currency payments; recruit qualified procurement and other experts; establish acceptable filing system; provide training on World Bank Procurement Regulations and STEP tool; conduct prior review of certain procurement activities; leverage AfDB project experience; periodic World Bank fiduciary reviews and on-site transaction sampling

identified_risks.risk_description: Low risk overall. No civil works or land acquisition. Potential risks from inadequate transparency and stakeholder communication. TA activities may have downstream environmental and social impacts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Prepare Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment (SESA); implement Stakeholder Engagement Plan with culturally appropriate feedback mechanisms; ensure transparent communication using appropriate language and media; establish Grievance Redress Mechanism; monitor environmental and social aspects during implementation

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (Relevant)
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure (Relevant)
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (Relevant)
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (Relevant)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level GRM established; communities and individuals can submit complaints to project-level mechanisms or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS); access to World Bank's independent Accountability Mechanism including Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service; complaints can be submitted at any time after concerns brought to Bank Management attention
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender gaps identified: only 4% of women have financial accounts vs. 5.8% overall; 82% of enterprises owned by males; only 18% female representation in BoSS senior and middle management (162 female staff out of 535 total); 2.2 million girls out of school. Project will: develop gender-inclusive Financial Sector Development Strategy with gender-sensitive KPIs; conduct gender analysis in institutional assessment; develop Gender Strategy for BoSS; ensure equitable access to training for female staff; target women for middle management development; develop gender bias workshops; establish talent pool linking trained women to employment/mentorship opportunities; establish monitoring system for sex-disaggregated financial sector data; target 64 women (32% of 200) for SSIBF training
vulnerable_groups: Women and girls (2.2 million out of school; limited financial inclusion; underrepresented in formal employment and financial sector)
Poor and extremely poor (80% of 13.3 million population in extreme poverty)
Unbanked population (94.2% of adult population without financial accounts)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Incorporates flexibility to adapt to volatile political and economic environment. Supports core institution (central bank) critical for crisis management and recovery. Aligned with Revitalized Agreement on Resolution of Conflict (R-ARCSS) institutional reform objectives. Supports financial sector stability which is essential for post-conflict recovery and private sector-led growth. Project design reflects lessons from FCV environments including need for simpler designs, achievable interventions, strong government commitment, and coordination with all stakeholders. Supports peace dividend through financial sector development enabling economic opportunities
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan classified as fragile state with multiple fragility factors: (1) Prolonged conflict legacy (2013-2018 civil war) with accumulated GDP loss of US$81.1 billion; (2) Weak institutions with limited capacity across government; (3) Elevated violence in several areas despite formal peace agreement; (4) Widespread poverty (80% in extreme poverty); (5) Food insecurity (60% of population after 2022 floods); (6) Displacement crisis (2.2 million IDPs, 2.2 million refugees); (7) Weak governance and transparency; (8) Non-diversified economy dependent on oil (89% of loans to government); (9) Climate vulnerability with recurring floods and droughts; (10) Weak financial sector trust and intermediation; (11) Limited human capital development; (12) Weak public financial management. Project addresses institutional fragility by strengthening central bank capacity
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security situation remains volatile with intercommunal conflicts persisting. Project implementation may be affected by security incidents. Implementation support missions may face access constraints. Staff security and safety considerations for project personnel. Risk of project delays due to security-related disruptions. Mitigation through flexible project design, regular coordination, and adaptive management. Project does not involve field-based activities in insecure areas; primarily office-based institutional strengthening in Juba
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Approximately 2.2 million internally displaced persons and 2.2 million South Sudanese refugees residing in neighboring countries. Project indirectly supports displaced and refugee populations through financial sector development that could facilitate remittances (baseline US$1.20 billion in 2022, projected to grow to US$1.39 billion by 2026 with project support). Improved financial sector stability and banking services could enhance remittance flows to displaced families. Project includes gender considerations relevant to displaced women and girls. No direct displacement or resettlement impacts from project activities
